abstract.p: A small article exercising the conversion of PreTeXt to XSL Formatting Objects, rendered as an accessible PDF by Apache FOP. It grows in concert with the capabilities of the conversion.

section.label: section-prose
section.title: Prose
section.p: This is the first paragraph of a minimal article, used to exercise the conversion of PreTeXt source to XSL Formatting Objects, and then to a PDF by Apache FOP. A paragraph is the fundamental unit of prose, so it is the first element implemented, and this one is long enough to demonstrate line-breaking and justification.

A second paragraph demonstrates inter-paragraph spacing. It contains some inline markup, such as emphasized text and a defined term, rendered with italic and bold fonts. A footnoteThe footnote drops to the bottom of the page, below a separator rule. hangs off this sentence.

section.label: section-inline
section.title: Inline Markup
section.p: Semantic markup, mostly by font: emphasis, a defined term, an alert, a lingua aliena, the book A First Course in Linear Algebra, the paper On Computable Numbers, and the species Quercus garryana.
Edits as inserted, deleted, and stale text. Verbatim text such as print("Hello, world"), and markup-language fragments like section, mdash, and xml:id.
Quotations, double and single. Dashes for ranges, 20232024, and for asideslike this one. An ellipsis and a few characters: 98.6, 35, , , and the logo itself.
Abbreviations render plainly: an acronym like SCUBA, an initialism like XML, and Latin abbreviations, , this very sentence.
Keyboard keys, named like and , or verbatim like q, render visibly boxed.
External references are active links: the PreTeXt website, the bare URL , and an address for writing, info@pretextbook.org. Cross-references, like one to ahead, are internal links.

section.label: section-lists
section.title: Lists
section.p:
An ordered list, with a nested unordered list, interrupts this paragraph.
A first list item, unstructured.
A second list item, structured, with a nested unordered list.
A bulleted item.
Another, with some emphasis.
A third item, to show the numbering continues.
and then the paragraph concludes.

A list may have an author-specified marker, such as Roman numerals.
One item.
A second item.
A description list pairs titles with explanations.

li.title: Kohlrabi
li.p: A cultivar of wild cabbage, with a swollen stem.

section.label: section-images
section.title: Images
section.p: An externally produced image, a right triangle, follows at half of the available width.

A born-in-source image, authored in syntax, follows. It is generated to SVG, which Apache FOP embeds directly; the diagram carries an intrinsic width and height, so it occupies only the vertical space it needs.

image.shortdescription: the decaying oscillation that solves a damped harmonic oscillator, with its derivative

section.label: section-tables
section.title: Tables and Figures
section.p: A small table of tree data follows, with a header row, rules, and varied column alignment.
A side-by-side arranges panels horizontally: an image, beside a list of the sides of the pictured triangle, with the bottoms of the two panels aligned.
Some prose separates the bare side-by-side above from the captioned figure below, so the two layout devices read as distinct.

figure.label: figure-triangle
figure.caption: A right triangle, now wrapped in a captioned figure.

section.label: section-verbatim
section.title: Verbatim
section.p:
Code display interrupts a paragraph,
s = "Hello, World!" print(s)
and then it continues.

program.code: total = 0 for i in range(1, 11): total = total + i print(total)

section.p: A console session, with prompt, bold input, and plain output.
A Sage cell, input boxed, output following.

remark.label: remark-placeholders
remark.title: Blocks Have Headings
remark.p: This remark is a basic titled block, with a heading assembled from its type, its number, and its title.

statement.p: The hypotenuse of a right triangle is the side opposite the right angle.
If a right triangle has legs a and b, and hypotenuse c, then a^2 + b^2 = c^2.

proof.p: Dissect a square with side length a + b in two different ways, and account for the areas.

solution.p: The left side factors as (x-2)(x-3), so the solutions are x = 2 and x = 3.

statement.p:
An integer n is even if and only if n^2 is even, a fact worth a reference number,
2 \mid n \iff 4 \mid n^2
which earns a decorative tag instead.

case.p: If n = 2k, then n^2 = 4k^2, so n^2 is even.
If n is odd, say n = 2k+1, then n^2 = 4k^2 + 4k + 1 is odd; the contrapositive completes the case.

paragraphs.label: paragraphs-history
paragraphs.title: A Little History
paragraphs.p: The lightweight paragraphs division has just a title, run in to this paragraph. Cross-references work too: see , , and , all referenced from . Even the starred equation answers to its decoration.

introduction.p: A subsection exercises a deeper heading, and a two-level number.

subsubsection.label: subsubsection-depth
subsubsection.title: More Depth
subsubsection.p: A subsubsection is the deepest traditional division, with a three-level number.

subsubsection.label: subsubsection-deeper
subsubsection.title: Deeper Still
subsubsection.p: A second subsubsection confirms that the numbering increments within the subsection.

conclusion.p: A subsection may also close with a conclusion, set after its subsubsections.

section.label: section-projects
section.title: Projects and Checkpoints
section.p: An inline exercise is a checkpoint, numbered with the blocks.

statement.p: A right triangle has legs 5 and 12. How long is the hypotenuse?
Compute the area of the 3-4-5 right triangle.

section.label: section-quotations
section.title: Quotations, Verse, Quantities
section.p: Fill in the blank: the answer is here. And a quantity with units: the speed limit is 100.

blockquote.p: The grand thing is to be able to reason backwards.

introduction.p: A worksheet is a printout, so an exercise may request workspaceblank space below, where a reader works. The space is elastic: it can stretch to fill out the printout's page, or surrender a bit when the page is crowded.

statement.p: Add the first ten positive integers.
Now multiply them. This deserves the larger workspace.
This exercise begins an authored second page. Formulate a conjecture relating the two preceding answers, with plenty of room to think.

conclusion.p: The conclusion of the worksheet follows the final workspace.

references.label: references-development
references.biblio: R. A. Beezer, A First Course in Linear Algebra, 3rd Edition, Congruent Press, 2012.
T. W. Judson, Abstract Algebra: Theory and Applications, Orthogonal Publishing, 2024.

colophon.label: colophon
colophon.p: This article was authored in, and produced with, , rendered by Apache FOP by way of XSL Formatting Objects.
